SOURCE CHANNEL: Web Portal
SUBMISSION PORTAL REF: portal-inbound-auth-4410

MESSAGE BODY:
CardioMed Medical Affairs Desk,

I am a staff pharmacist at Boston Medical Center currently verifying an inpatient medical order entry for a patient who has been prescribed your product CholoClear-X.

The physician's order lists a titration schedule that reaches 100mg daily. However, our internal clinical pharmacy guidelines state that the maximum recommended maintenance amount on the standard product label is 50mg daily. Could your team please confirm the exact approved package insert ceiling guidelines for standard adult hypercholesterolemia administration?

I want to cross-reference our internal database parameters with your official documentation before dispensing the medication to the ward this afternoon.

Thank you,
Jonathan Vance, PharmD
Boston Medical Center Pharmacy Dept
Email: jvance@bostonmed.org | Phone: 617-555-0122

fields.value: [self-consistency: 3/3 Serious] Multiple ICH E2A serious criteria are met: (b) life-threatening — the patient presented in profound anaphylactic shock with a systolic BP of 62 mmHg and severe respiratory distress, constituting an immediate risk of death at the time of the event; (c) inpatient hospitalization — she was admitted to the Cardiac ICU requiring vasopressor support and continuous airway monitoring; and the IME exception also applies, as aggressive emergency interventions (IM epinephrine, IV corticosteroids, fluid resuscitation) were required to prevent death.
fields.evidence: intramuscular epinephrine, high-dose intravenous corticosteroids, and massive crystalloid fluid resuscitation
fields.reason: Aggressive emergency interventions support the IME criterion to prevent death

cases.narrative: severe upper airway throat swelling; profound difficulty breathing; widespread confluent urticarial hives; critical hemodynamic blood pressure crash (systolic dropped to 62 mmHg); anaphylactic shock.
cases.sourceText:
FAX INBOUND REFLINE: FX-CARDIOLOGY-STJUDE-2026
DATE: JUNE 06, 2026
FROM: St. Jude Heart & Vascular Center, Emergency Consult Service
TO: BioPharma Corporate Pharmacovigilance & Medical Affairs

CRITICAL ADVERSE EVENT REPORT — SPONTANEOUS SURVEILLANCE

Attn: Medical Information Director,

I am writing to formally log a severe, life-threatening suspected adverse drug reaction regarding your oral small-molecule agent, CholoClear-X (50mg formulation).

The patient, a 58-year-old Caucasian female (Initials: R.K.), was prescribed CholoClear-X on an outpatient basis for refractory hypercholesterolemia. This morning, approximately 30 minutes after ingesting her second scheduled dose, she experienced an explosive, acute systemic hypersensitivity cascade.

Upon emergency arrival at our facility, clinical presentation included severe upper airway throat swelling, profound diff1cultly breath1ng [sic], widespread confluent urticarial hives, and a critical hemodynamic blood pressure crash (systolic dropped to 62 mmHg, presenting in profound anaphylactic shock).

Aggressive emergency intervention was initiated immediately with intramuscular epinephrine, high-dose intravenous corticosteroids, and massive crystalloid fluid resuscitation. Due to severe respiratory distress and hemodynamic instability, she was urgently transferred and admitted to our Cardiac Intensive Care Unit (ICU Bed 4) for continuous vasopressor support and airway monitoring. She is currently stabilized but remains in critical condition. No concomitant medications were introduced prior to the event, and she has no history of clinical trial enrollment.

Please log this case immediately. Address any inquiries or requests for follow-up to my desk.

Reporting Cardiologist:
Dr. Samuel Brennan, MD, FACC
Senior Attending Cardiologist, Registry ID: #NET_HCP_77412
St. Jude Heart & Vascular Center, 500 Harrison Ave, Chicago, IL 60618
Office Phone: 312-555-0177 | Secure Email: sbrennan@stjudeheart.org
cases.classEvidence: CRITICAL ADVERSE EVENT REPORT — SPONTANEOUS SURVEILLANCE
cases.classReason: Document header explicitly labels this as an Adverse Event Report

cases.sourceText:
FAX TRANSMISSION -- RECEIVED VIA INBOUND NODE 4
TIMESTAMP: 2026-06-08 11:14 UTC

TO: CardioMed Medical Info Team
FROM: St. Jude Clinical Pharmacy Dept
REQUESTOR: Sarah Jenkins, RPh (Lead Pharmacist)
CONTACT EMAIL: s.jenkins@stjudehealth.org

MESSAGE BODY:
Dear Medical Affairs,

We have a dosing question regarding your commercial lipid-lowering tablet, CholoClear-X (50mg oral).

We have an adult patient with refractory hypercholesterolemia who is tolerating the standard 50mg once-daily dose well but has not yet reached target LDL. Could you confirm whether the approved package insert supports titrating CholoClear-X above 50mg per day (for example to 75mg or 100mg daily), or is 50mg the maximum approved adult maintenance dose?

We want to stay strictly within the approved label before adjusting this patient's regimen. Please point us to the relevant Dosage and Administration section.

Regards,
Sarah Jenkins, RPh
St. Jude Clinical Pharmacy
Phone: 901-555-0144

cases.classRationale: The document is best classified as a Product Quality Complaint because it reports a physical/environmental integrity concern (heat excursion compromising a sealed product batch), though it competes modestly with Formulation & Excipient Information since the underlying question touches on storage-stability data — a technical formulation characteristic — and with On-Label Medical Inquiry since the PI's storage section is referenced.

cases.sourceText:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Email
MESSAGE ID: <msg-2026-0608-7741@clevelandclinic.org>

MESSAGE BODY:
Dear Medical Affairs,

We have a storage-stability question regarding your commercial lipid-lowering tablet, CholoClear-X (50mg oral).

A sealed bottle of CholoClear-X tablets was inadvertently left in a clinic delivery vehicle over a long weekend, where the internal temperature is estimated to have reached approximately 40 degrees C for around 30 hours, exceeding the labeled storage range.

The How Supplied / Storage and Handling section of the package insert notes a recommended storage temperature but does not specify how to handle a transient excursion of this magnitude. Could you confirm whether this heat excursion requires us to quarantine and discard the bottle, or whether stability is still acceptable for dispensing?

Regards,
Priya Nair, PharmD
Cleveland Clinic
Email: p.nair@clevelandclinic.org | Phone: 216-555-0133
cases.classEvidence: storage-stability question regarding your commercial lipid-lowering tablet
cases.classReason: The inquiry concerns potential product degradation due to a temperature excursion, which is a product quality concern, not a complaint about adverse effects…

SOURCE CHANNEL: Web Portal
SUBMISSION PORTAL REF: portal-inbound-clinical-3321

MESSAGE BODY:
CardioMed Medical Information Team,

I am currently verifying a titration protocol for an adult patient with severe, refractory hypercholesterolemia.

According to section 2.2 of your official CholoClear-X package insert, the maximum approved maintenance dosage ceiling is explicitly capped at 50mg daily. However, our department head wants to implement an aggressive titration step that pushes the patient up to 75mg daily over a 4-week period if target LDL-C levels aren't met.

Could your medical team provide any available clinical trial datasets, pharmacokinetic profiles, or safety clearance documentation regarding adult administrations exceeding the standard 50mg label boundary up to 75mg? We need to establish if this dose adjustment carries an unacceptable risk profile before initiating the ward protocol.

This is an unsolicited inquiry from our practice.

Thank you,
Dr. Keith Sorensen
Metro Health Cardiovascular Group
Email: k.sorensen@metrocardio.org | Phone: 212-555-0199

MESSAGE BODY:
CardioMed Global Medical Information Desk,

I am an outpatient clinic coordinator at Cleveland Clinic reviewing the formulary updates for our cardiovascular risk management programs.

One of our attending cardiologists submitted an internal request to compare standard initiation dosages for patient transitions. Can your medical team confirm if your master package insert provides a specific protocol for switching a patient directly from a 20mg dose of LipidShield-PRO to your standard maintenance titration arm?

If you have an approved clinical trial summary or an official consensus statement regarding direct titration equivalence during therapeutic crossover, please forward the documentation to our team as soon as possible.

Sincerely,
Olivia Bennett, BSN, RN
Cleveland Clinic Cardiovascular Center
Email: o.bennett@clevelandclinic.org | Phone: 216-555-0188

MESSAGE BODY:
ATTENTION: CHIEF PROCUREMENT OFFICER & MEDICAL OPERATIONS DIRECTOR

Are your clinical groups spending thousands too much every quarter on outdated pharmacy analytics software and manual patient charting trackers?

Our team at Enterprise Health Solutions LLC just launched MedAI-Track v4.0, a premier cloud platform optimized for tracking tier-1 blockbusters like CholoClear-X and other leading lipid-lowering assets. We guarantee our automated dashboards reduce your data compilation overhead by up to 35% within thirty days.

Click below to access our executive whitepaper and arrange a mandatory 15-minute presentation with our regional sales VP this week. Act now to lock in our Q2 volume discount pricing!

This is a commercial announcement. To remove your address from our marketing list, reply UNSUBSCRIBE.

cases.sourceText:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Email Inbound Gateway
PRIMARY DISPATCH LOG: info-reply@cardiomedglobal.com

MESSAGE BODY:
Hi there,

My local pharmacy down on 4th street told me to message your support email directly. I was looking through your public corporate website to try and find the downloadable consumer instructions or a patient voucher coupon for your medication CholoClear-X, but I couldn't find the link anywhere on the main page. Can someone send me the direct website link or an online brochure where I can download that?

Also, while I have you, I wanted to ask if it's normal to get a strange burning sensation in my chest and an irregular, racing heartbeat about an hour after I take the pill? It has happened every morning since I started the new prescription last Thursday, and it makes me feel quite dizzy for a few hours, so I have to sit down at my desk. My doctor is out of town until next week, so I just wanted to read your official patient safety booklet online to see if those are standard issues or if I should stop taking the medication altogether.

Thank you for your help,
Marcus Vance
Home Address: 542 Applewood Lane, Austin, TX
Personal Mobile: 512-555-0144

cases.sourceText:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Email Inbound Gateway
PRIMARY DISPATCH LOG: safety-intake@cardiomedglobal.com

MESSAGE BODY:
To CardioMed Drug Safety,

I am reporting a serious adverse event in one of my patients, a 64-year-old male, who has been taking your product CholoClear-X for hypercholesterolemia. Approximately three weeks after starting therapy he developed progressive jaundice, dark urine, and severe right-upper-quadrant pain. Labs showed markedly elevated transaminases (ALT 880, AST 740) and bilirubin. He was admitted to our hospital for acute drug-induced liver injury and remains inpatient under monitoring. His only concomitant medication is lisinopril for hypertension.

Please confirm receipt for your safety database.

Nathan Cole, MD
Riverside Hepatology Associates
Email: ncole@riversidehep.org | Phone: 415-555-0188

SOURCE CHANNEL: Email Inbound Gateway
PRIMARY DISPATCH LOG: patient-support@cardiomedglobal.com

MESSAGE BODY:
Hello,

I started taking CholoClear-X about a week ago for my cholesterol. I've noticed some mild muscle aches in my legs and shoulders — nothing severe, I haven't seen a doctor or gone to hospital, and the aches mostly went away after a couple of days. I just wanted to let you know in case it's a known side effect. I'm still taking the medication.

Thanks,
Diane Foster
Personal email: diane.foster88@gmail.com
Mobile: 503-555-0173

cases.sourceText:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Email Inbound Gateway
PRIMARY DISPATCH LOG: safety-intake@cardiomedglobal.com

MESSAGE BODY:
Hi CardioMed,

I'm a community pharmacist. This morning a customer came to my counter quite distressed — they said they had developed significant facial and lip swelling a few hours after taking CholoClear-X, which they'd just picked up. Before I could take down their name or any details, they rushed off to an urgent care clinic. I don't have their information, but I felt I should report the reaction in case it's important for your safety monitoring.

Regards,
Gregory Pratt, RPh
Cornerstone Community Pharmacy
Email: gpratt@cornerstonerx.com

cases.sourceText:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Email Inbound Gateway
PRIMARY DISPATCH LOG: safety-intake@cardiomedglobal.com

MESSAGE BODY:
To the CardioMed Safety Team,

I am reporting a fatal event. A 72-year-old female patient of mine, who had been on CholoClear-X for approximately two months, was found unresponsive at home and could not be resuscitated. She had reported episodes of palpitations and lightheadedness in the days prior. While other cardiac factors may be involved, given the temporal association I am reporting this death for your safety assessment. Concomitant medications were metoprolol and apixaban.

Please acknowledge receipt.

Maria Santos, MD, FACC
Lakeside Cardiology Institute
Email: msantos@lakesidecardio.org | Phone: 312-555-0166
cases.classEvidence: I am reporting this death for your safety assessment
cases.classReason: Reporter explicitly submits the event as a safety report, classifying it as an adverse event report

MESSAGE BODY:
CardioMed Medical Information,

I am a clinical pharmacist reviewing a new start for CholoClear-X. Could you confirm whether CholoClear-X is contraindicated in patients with active hepatic disease or persistently elevated serum transaminases? I want to verify against the approved label before we dispense.

Thank you,
Priya Nair, PharmD
Cleveland Clinic
Email: pnair@clevelandclinic.org | Phone: 216-555-0140

CardioMed Medical Affairs,

Best regards,
Aris Thorne, MD, PhD
St. Jude Research Hospital

cases.sourceText:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Email

Dear CardioMed Medical Affairs,

I have a 61-year-old patient with homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia who has exhausted every approved therapy and is not eligible for any open clinical trial. I am writing to request compassionate-use access to your investigational agent CMG-401 under an expanded access arrangement. The patient's condition is rapidly deteriorating and we consider this their last option.

Please advise on how to proceed.

Dr. Helena Marsh, MD
Director, Lipid Disorders Clinic, Mass General, Boston, MA
cases.classEvidence: request compassionate-use access to your investigational agent CMG-401 under an expanded access arrangement
cases.classReason: Email explicitly requests both compassionate-use access and an expanded access arrangement

SOURCE CHANNEL: Email

Dear CardioMed Scientific Affairs,

Our group at the University of Michigan would like to propose an investigator-initiated study evaluating CholoClear-X in patients with chronic kidney disease and mixed dyslipidemia. We are seeking study drug supply and partial grant funding to run this independent single-center trial. Attached is a one-page concept; we'd welcome your support.

Prof. Ananya Rao, MD PhD
Division of Nephrology, University of Michigan

Hello,

I'd like to submit a concept for a Phase-IV real-world outcomes study of CholoClear-X in a community cardiology setting. We would design and run it ourselves but would need CardioMed to sponsor study drug and a modest research grant. How do I formally submit an IIT proposal for review?

Dr. Trevor Boone, MD
Coastal Heart Institute, San Diego, CA

cases.narrative: The document is a clear Product Quality Complaint — a pharmacist reports physical defects (cracked and discolored tablets) in a specific lot of CholoClear-X (CCX-2451A), explicitly states no patient has been harmed, and requests a replacement and return instructions; no adverse event, clinical question, or other competing class is present.
cases.sourceText:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Email

CardioMed Quality / Medical Info,

We received a bottle of CholoClear-X 50mg tablets in our pharmacy where roughly a third of the tablets are visibly cracked and several are discolored (brownish spotting). No patient has taken any of these — we pulled the bottle immediately. Lot number on the bottle is CCX-2451A. We'd like a replacement and to report the defect.

No patient harm. Please advise on return.

Grace Lim, RPh
Harborview Hospital Pharmacy, Seattle, WA
cases.classEvidence: roughly a third of the tablets are visibly cracked and several are discolored (brownish spotting)

During stock intake our nurse noticed a vial of CholoClear-X Injectable with a broken tamper seal and a small floating particle visible in the solution. No patient was exposed.

Nurse Manager, St. Anne's Regional Medical Center

SOURCE CHANNEL: Email (Regulatory Inbox)

U.S. FOOD AND DRUG ADMINISTRATION
Center for Drug Evaluation and Research

RE: Information Request — CholoClear-X (NDA 21-XXXX)

This letter constitutes an official request for information following our recent inspection. Please provide, within 15 business days, your complete post-marketing adverse event reconciliation logs and the associated SOPs referenced during the inspection. Direct your response to the assigned project manager.

Office of Compliance, CDER
U.S. FDA

cases.sourceText:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Email (Regulatory Inbox)

MINISTRY OF HEALTH — Pharmaceutical Inspectorate

NOTICE OF AUDIT

This notice informs CardioMed of a scheduled good-distribution-practice audit focused on cold-chain and storage-temperature controls for CholoClear-X Injectable. Please make available your temperature-excursion records for the past 12 months. An inspector will contact your QA lead to arrange dates.

National Medicines Regulatory Authority

SOURCE CHANNEL: Email

Hi, quick question on CholoClear-X dosing — is 50mg still the right maintenance dose for an elderly patient? Also, my patient mentioned she felt dizzy and a bit lightheaded for a couple of hours after her dose yesterday. Probably nothing, but thought I'd mention it.

Dr. Carmen Ruiz, MD
Greenfield Family Practice
cases.classEvidence: is 50mg still the right maintenance dose for an elderly patient
cases.classReason: The question concerns standard labeled dosing guidance, making it an on-label medical inquiry

fields.label: Seriousness Rationale
fields.value: [self-consistency: 0/3 Serious] None of the 5 ICH E2A criteria are met (no death, life-threatening risk, hospitalization, disability/incapacity, or congenital anomaly), and the IME exception does not apply as the localized skin rash (redness, raised, itchy) on the forearm did not jeopardize the patient nor require medical/surgical intervention to prevent a serious outcome based on what the text states.
fields.evidence: red, raised, itchy rash where it contacted my skin
fields.reason: Localized skin rash on forearm with no mention of death, hospitalization, disability, or life-threatening risk supports non-serious classification

cases.narrative: red, raised, itchy rash on forearm.
cases.sourceText:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Email

Reporting a problem: my ArthriFree pre-filled syringe cracked while I was injecting it this morning, and the medicine sprayed onto my forearm. Within an hour I developed a red, raised, itchy rash where it contacted my skin. The device clearly malfunctioned but I also reacted to it.

— Sandra Mills (patient)
cases.classEvidence: I also reacted to it
cases.classReason: Patient reports both a device malfunction and an adverse reaction, qualifying as an Adverse Event Report

cases.banner: The AI couldn't produce a valid record — please review manually.

cases.narrative: The caller describes a specific, unexpected clinical event (extreme somnolence/difficult to arouse) in a patient following initiation of CholoClear-X, making this a clear Adverse Event Report; a secondary On-Label Medical Inquiry signal exists (asking whether the symptom is drug-related), but the safety event is the dominant and most critical classification.
cases.sourceText:
SOURCE CHANNEL: Call Center (transcribed)

Caller: I'm worried about my mom. She started those new cholesterol pills, CholoClear-X, two days ago. Yesterday she took her dose and then just... fell asleep at the kitchen table in the middle of the afternoon, and we could barely wake her. She's never like that. Is that from the medicine?
cases.classEvidence: she took her dose and then just... fell asleep at the kitchen table in the middle of the afternoon, and we could barely wake her
cases.classReason: Caller describes an unexpected harmful reaction (extreme sedation/unrousability) after taking the drug CholoClear-X, constituting an adverse event report
